source.title: Distinctive alterations in the functional anatomy of the cerebral cortex in pain-sensitized osteoarthritis and fibromyalgia patients

confidence_rationale: Direct evidence that pain sensitisation -- the clinical face of central gain enhancement / multiplicative amplification on the body-state stream -- presents with distinct cortical functional-connectivity signatures in fibromyalgia (broadly distributed) versus pain-sensitised osteoarthritis (insula-localised). This dissociation supports SD-048 Source 2 (sensitisation spikes as multiplicative amplification of the interoceptive readout) by showing that sensitisation does change the functional state of the cortical interoceptive substrate at the connectivity level. Source quality moderate-high (n=31 + n=38 plus replication n=34 + n=63, fMRI seed-to-voxel functional connectivity). Mapping fidelity moderate -- the paper studies clinical chronic pain populations, not transient sensitisation events of the kind SD-048 simulates with Poisson-onset/exponential-decay dynamics. Transfer risk moderate-to-high: the underlying mechanism (central gain enhancement) is the same architectural family, but timescale differs.
failure_signatures: If SD-048 sensitisation spikes produce no detectable comparator-circuit signature, while empirical clinical sensitisation reliably does, the substrate-level analogue is failing to capture the structural feature being modelled.
If REE's comparator only reads SD-048 sensitisation events as additional damage rather than as gain changes, the multiplicative-amplification mechanism the paper documents would not transfer.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Pain-sensitised osteoarthritis (pOA, n=31, replicated n=34) and fibromyalgia (FM, n=38, replicated n=63) patients showed distinct cortical functional-connectivity signatures relative to controls. pOA showed weaker local connectivity in the insula, consistent with reduced neural activity following repeated activation -- compatible with metabolic recovery cost on a peripherally-originated chronic pain. FM showed weaker connectivity broadly across sensorimotor cortex, compatible with central-gain enhancement: low sensory tolerance from over-amplification of central reception, possibly compensating weak proprioceptive input. The paper interprets the FM pattern as a central gain enhancement signature.
mapping.ree_translation: SD-048 Source 2 (sensitisation spikes) implements transient multiplicative amplification of `harm_obs_a` -- the substrate-level analogue of central gain enhancement on the affective harm stream. Pujol et al.'s FM pattern is the clinical end of this mechanism: chronic over-amplification of central interoceptive reception. The substrate-level prediction is that, when SD-048 Source 2 fires, the interoceptive comparator should detect a gain-style perturbation distinct from a damage-style perturbation. The paper supports the broader claim that gain-style perturbation has its own observable signature on the cortical interoceptive substrate.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper studies chronic clinical populations with months-to-years sensitisation history; SD-048 Source 2 simulates transient sensitisation events with ~15-step half-life inside an episode. The structural shape (multiplicative amplification of body-state readout, central gain enhancement) is the shared feature; the timescale is not. Mapping the chronic-population signature onto a per-episode Poisson process is principled but transfer risk is moderate. Additionally, the paper does not separate sensitisation spike vs sustained gain elevation -- both would map onto SD-048 Source 2 under different rate / decay parameter regimes. The paper supports the broader category, not the specific Poisson dynamics.
mapping.source_context: Resting-state fMRI seed-to-voxel functional connectivity, two case-control comparisons (pOA, FM), each with replication sample. n=31+34 pain-sensitised osteoarthritis, n=38+63 fibromyalgia. Spanish multi-site collaboration.

confidence_components.notes: Sample sizes adequate, replication included. Mapping fidelity moderate because the paper documents central gain enhancement as a chronic clinical feature, while SD-048 Source 2 implements transient sensitisation spikes. Transfer risk moderate-to-high because of timescale mismatch between chronic clinical sensitisation and per-episode Poisson events.
